name: Zonules.com Claim Registry
note: Every scientific claim is supported by a registered source, marked as conceptual interpretation, marked as internal framework, or excluded.

claims.statement: The zonules of Zinn are fine fibrous strands that suspend the crystalline lens from the ciliary body.
The zonular apparatus, also called the suspensory ligament of the lens, holds the lens in centered geometric tension.
Tension transmitted through the zonules allows the ciliary muscle to change lens shape during accommodation, shifting focus across distances.
The structure is named after Johann Gottfried Zinn, the 18th-century anatomist who described it.
The zonules are the literal instance of the suspension layer (FIO-01): focus is produced by hidden structural tension, not by the lens alone.
The ciliary body is a ring of tissue behind the iris that contains the ciliary muscle and gives rise to the zonular fibers.
Contraction of the ciliary muscle reduces tension on the zonules, allowing the elastic lens to become more convex for near focus.
The ciliary body also produces aqueous humor, the fluid that fills the anterior segment of the eye.
The ciliary body is the force source of the suspension layer (FIO-01): it does not focus light but governs the tension that makes focus adjustable.
The crystalline lens is a transparent, biconvex structure suspended behind the iris that refracts light to help form an image on the retina.
The lens is elastic and changes curvature during accommodation.
The lens is enclosed in an elastic capsule into which the zonular fibers insert.
The lens is the focal element of the suspension system: present but unable to focus reliably without zonular tension.
Accommodation is the process by which the eye changes its optical power to maintain a clear image of objects at varying distances.
During accommodation for near vision, the ciliary muscle contracts, zonular tension decreases, and the lens becomes more spherical.
Accommodative ability typically declines with age, a condition known as presbyopia.
Accommodation is the operational expression of FIS-2: focus integrity maintained across an intended operating range.
The lens capsule is a transparent, elastic basement-membrane envelope that surrounds the crystalline lens.
The zonular fibers insert into the lens capsule around the lens equator, transmitting their tension to the lens through the capsule.
The elasticity of the capsule helps mold the lens into a more curved shape when zonular tension is released during accommodation.
The lens capsule is the transmission interface of the suspension layer: the surface through which hidden tension becomes a change in focal geometry.
Visual perception is the process by which the brain organizes and interprets visual input into a stable, meaningful representation of the environment.
Perception is constructive: the visual system actively organizes input rather than passively recording it.
Selective attention determines which parts of the visual field are processed in detail.
Perception is the Layer 02 instance of the suspension thesis: attention holds a focal interpretation in place the way the zonules hold the lens.
Machine vision is the field of methods by which artificial systems detect, classify, localize, and interpret content in images and video.
Machine vision systems can produce correct detections while failing to interpret a scene correctly.
Establishing the origin and authenticity of an image is addressed by content provenance standards rather than by detection alone.
Machine vision is the Layer 03 instance of the focus-integrity thesis: calibration and grounding play the suspension role.
Selective attention is the mechanism by which the visual system prioritizes some stimuli for detailed processing while suppressing others.
Attention can be directed voluntarily (top-down) or captured involuntarily (bottom-up) by salient stimuli.
Attentional capacity is limited, so not all of the visual field can be processed in detail at the same time.
Selective attention is the perceptual suspension mechanism (FIO-01): it holds a focal target in place.
Figure-ground organization is the perceptual process of segregating a scene into objects (figure) and background (ground).
Figure-ground assignment can be ambiguous, as demonstrated by reversible figures.
Gestalt principles describe how the visual system groups elements into coherent figures.
Figure-ground separation is the perceptual instance of signal-noise integrity (FIO-03).
Deepfakes are synthetic media in which a person's likeness is generated or manipulated using machine-learning methods.
Detection methods analyze artifacts, inconsistencies, or statistical signatures to classify media as authentic or synthetic.
Detection alone does not establish origin; content provenance approaches record an image's source and edit history.
Deepfake detection is a focus-integrity problem of provenance (FIO-04).
Image provenance is the recorded history of where an image came from and how it was modified.
Content provenance standards attach tamper-evident metadata to media to support authenticity verification.
Provenance complements detection by establishing origin rather than inferring manipulation from the pixels alone.
Image provenance is the infrastructure of provenance integrity (FIO-04).
The cornea is the transparent, avascular anterior surface of the eye and provides approximately two-thirds of the eye's total refractive power.
Corneal transparency depends on the precise arrangement and hydration of collagen fibrils in the stroma.
The cornea is a focus-integrity element of signal quality (FIO-03): its transparency determines whether the optical signal is clear before it reaches the lens.
The retina is the light-sensitive tissue lining the posterior interior of the eye, containing photoreceptors and multiple layers of neural processing cells.
The retina converts incident light into neural signals through phototransduction.
The retina is the anatomical site where focus integrity is decided (FIO-05).

Photoreceptors are specialized retinal cells that transduce light into neural signals; rods are sensitive to low light and motion, cones are concentrated at the fovea and detect color and fine detail.
The highest density of cone photoreceptors is at the fovea.
Photoreceptor density and distribution determine the spatial resolution and luminance sensitivity available to the visual system.
Photoreceptors are the signal-noise boundary of anatomy (FIO-03).
The optic nerve is the bundle of approximately one million axons that carry retinal ganglion cell output from the eye to the lateral geniculate nucleus and primary visual cortex.
Damage to the optic nerve causes permanent loss of the visual field it carries.
The optic nerve is the structural conduit of vision (FIO-01): without its physical integrity, no signal reaches interpretation.
Perceptual constancy is the tendency to maintain a stable representation of an object despite variations in viewing angle, distance, illumination, or context.
Classical forms include size constancy, shape constancy, and lightness constancy.
Constancy depends on the visual system's ability to discount conditions of observation and infer invariant properties.
Perceptual constancy is the perceptual analogue of interpretation integrity (FIS-5).
The Gestalt principles describe how the visual system organizes elements into perceptual wholes; proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, and figure-ground are the core grouping laws.
Gestalt organization reflects fundamental tendencies of the perceptual system to structure visual input into coherent units.
Failure of Gestalt organization produces an unstable or fragmented percept.
Gestalt principles are the structural grammar of perceptual signal separation (FIO-03).
Computer vision is the field concerned with enabling machines to extract meaningful information from digital images and video.
Modern computer vision systems use learned representations, with deep convolutional networks having become dominant for most recognition tasks.
Computer vision systems operate on pixel arrays; input signal quality directly affects downstream task performance.
Computer vision is the machine layer in which all five FIO failure classes can be instantiated.
Image classification is the task of assigning a categorical label to an image from a predefined set of classes.
Classification performance depends on the model's ability to learn discriminative features that generalize.
Distribution shift is a systematic source of classification failure even in high-accuracy systems.
Image classification is a focus-integrity problem of interpretation (FIO-05).
The iris is the pigmented muscular diaphragm that controls the size of the pupil.
The iris contains sphincter pupillae and dilator pupillae muscles allowing pupil constriction and dilation.
The iris is the accommodation layer of the eye's signal input (FIO-02).
The vitreous humor is the transparent, gel-like substance filling the posterior chamber, accounting for approximately two-thirds of the eye's total volume.
The vitreous humor provides the spatial separation between lens and retina required for the optical system to focus.
The vitreous humor is a structural element of focus integrity (FIO-01): it maintains the geometry of the optical system.
The macula is the central zone of the retina, approximately 5 mm in diameter, containing the highest concentration of cone photoreceptors.
The fovea at the center of the macula is the region of sharpest fixation.
The macula is the anatomical site of signal precision (FIO-03).
Depth perception is the ability to perceive relative distance from two-dimensional retinal images, achieved through binocular and monocular cues.
Binocular disparity is the primary cue for depth at close range; stereopsis is the neural process of resolving this disparity.
Monocular depth cues including perspective, familiar size, occlusion, and motion parallax allow depth inference from a single eye.
Depth perception is an interpretation problem (FIO-05): the system must construct a 3D representation from 2D signals.
Inattentional blindness is the failure to notice a fully visible but unexpected stimulus when attention is directed elsewhere.
Inattentional blindness demonstrates that conscious visual awareness requires attention as well as sensory input.
The probability of inattentional blindness increases when the unexpected stimulus is dissimilar to the attended target.
Inattentional blindness is a provenance failure (FIO-04): the input has no provenance in working memory.
Visual working memory temporarily stores and actively maintains visual information; its capacity is limited to approximately three to four objects.
Visual working memory enables comparison of a current scene with a recently stored representation.
The limited capacity of visual working memory creates predictable failures of comparison and change detection.
Visual working memory is the accommodation system of perception (FIO-02).
Object detection identifies and localizes object instances in an image, producing a category label and spatial boundary.
Object detection systems must generalize across scale, orientation, lighting, and occlusion variations.
False positives and false negatives are the symmetric failure modes of detection, controlled by a confidence threshold.
Object detection is an interpretation task (FIO-05): it requires correct categorization and correct localization.
Adversarial examples are inputs that have been imperceptibly perturbed to cause incorrect model outputs.
High accuracy on standard benchmarks does not guarantee robustness: a model can fail on inputs that appear normal to a human observer.
Adversarial robustness is a distinct property from standard accuracy and must be specifically engineered.
Adversarial examples are signal-noise failures (FIO-03): imperceptible perturbations defeat the signal-noise separation the model relies on.
The aqueous humor is the clear fluid filling the anterior and posterior chambers, supplying nutrients to the avascular cornea and lens.
Aqueous humor is continuously produced by the ciliary body and drained through the trabecular meshwork.
The aqueous humor is a structural maintenance element of focus integrity (FIO-01).
The primary visual cortex (V1) receives the principal visual input from the lateral geniculate nucleus and performs the first cortical stage of visual processing.
Neurons in V1 are tuned to edge orientation, spatial frequency, and direction of motion, decomposing the retinal image into elementary features.
The visual cortex is where interpretation begins (FIO-05): cortical failure produces a defect of interpretation rather than of sensing.

The pupillary light reflex is the automatic constriction of the pupil in response to increased light, mediated by a reflex arc involving the retina, optic nerve, pretectal nucleus, and iris sphincter.
The pupillary light reflex is consensual: light directed into one eye produces constriction in both pupils.
The pupillary light reflex is an automatic accommodation mechanism (FIO-02).
Sustained attention is the capacity to maintain focused attention on a task over an extended period.
Vigilance performance typically declines over time on monotonous monitoring tasks — the vigilance decrement.
Sustained attention is distinct from selective attention: selective attention concerns which stimulus is prioritized, sustained attention concerns maintaining that prioritization across time.
Sustained attention is the temporal dimension of perceptual suspension (FIO-01): the vigilance decrement is suspension failure across duration.
Change blindness is the failure to detect a substantial change when it coincides with a brief visual disruption.
Change blindness demonstrates that the visual system does not maintain a complete detailed scene representation.
Changes to currently attended objects are detected readily; changes to unattended objects frequently go unnoticed.
Change blindness is a provenance failure across time (FIO-04).
Visual saliency is the property by which some scene elements stand out and attract attention automatically, driven by local feature contrasts.
Bottom-up saliency operates rapidly and involuntarily, prioritizing locations before task-driven control is applied.
Computational saliency models predict where observers look by computing feature contrast maps.
Visual saliency is the signal-prioritization stage of perceptual signal separation (FIO-03).
Geometric calibration establishes the relationship between a camera's image coordinates and the 3D world.
Accurate calibration is a prerequisite for tasks depending on spatial measurement; calibration error propagates into every downstream geometric estimate.
Calibration can drift over time due to thermal, mechanical, or optical changes.
Calibration is the suspension system of machine vision (FIO-01): it holds the model's spatial reference frame in place.
Domain shift occurs when deployment input distribution differs from training distribution, degrading performance.
Common sources include changes in lighting, sensor characteristics, geographic context, and object appearance.
Domain adaptation and domain generalization address performance across distribution shift.
Domain shift is the accommodation failure of machine vision (FIO-02).
The fovea centralis is a shallow depression at the centre of the macula, approximately 1.5 mm in diameter, containing the highest concentration of cone photoreceptors in the eye.
At the foveal centre, overlying retinal layers are displaced laterally, leaving only cone photoreceptors to receive the focused image with minimal optical scatter.
The fovea contains no rod photoreceptors; it is entirely cone-mediated, conferring high-acuity, colour-sensitive vision in the central visual field.
The fovea is the anatomical zero-point of focus integrity (FIO-03): every optical element exists to deliver a clean signal to this location.
The optic chiasm is the X-shaped junction at the base of the brain where nasal retinal fibres from each optic nerve cross to the contralateral hemisphere; temporal fibres remain ipsilateral.
Compression of the optic chiasm produces bitemporal hemianopia, loss of both temporal visual fields, because the crossing nasal fibres are selectively damaged.
The optic chiasm is a suspension element of the visual pathway (FIO-01): it maintains the structural routing necessary for binocular signal integration.

Each optic tract carries post-chiasmatic visual fibres from the ipsilateral temporal retina and the contralateral nasal retina, representing the contralateral visual field, from the optic chiasm to the lateral geniculate nucleus.
The retinotopic organisation of the visual field is preserved through the optic tract: neighbouring fibres carry signals from neighbouring points in the visual field.
The optic tract is a suspension element of the visual pathway (FIO-01): a non-redundant structural conduit whose failure collapses signal delivery to the cortex.

The lateral geniculate nucleus is a layered thalamic relay that receives visual input from the optic tract and projects to the primary visual cortex, maintaining retinotopic organisation and segregating signals into magnocellular and parvocellular streams.
The LGN receives substantially more input from cortical feedback than from the retina, making it an active gating station that modulates signals based on attentional state and arousal.
The lateral geniculate nucleus is a suspension element of the visual pathway (FIO-01): it maintains the active signal channel between the retina and the visual cortex.
Binocular vision integrates input from two laterally separated eyes into a single percept; binocular disparity is the primary cue for stereoscopic depth at close range.
Binocular fusion requires the oculomotor system to maintain alignment of both eyes on the same point; misalignment produces diplopia or suppression of one eye's input.
Binocular rivalry results in perceptual alternation between incompatible images from the two eyes, demonstrating active perceptual selection.
Binocular vision is an interpretation problem (FIO-05): the system must construct a depth representation from the disparity between two signals.
The oculomotor system controls eye movement through six extraocular muscles per eye, producing saccades, smooth pursuit, vergence, and the vestibulo-ocular reflex to direct and stabilise gaze.
Vergence eye movements work together with lens accommodation and the pupillary light reflex as the accommodative near triad.
Saccadic suppression reduces visual sensitivity during saccades; the perceived stability of the visual world is a perceptual construction from fixation-period snapshots.
The oculomotor system defines the accommodation range of gaze (FIO-02): it sets the envelope of positions across which high-acuity fixation can be directed.

The sclera is the tough, fibrous outer coat of the eye forming approximately five-sixths of the globe's outer surface, maintaining the eye's spherical shape and providing extraocular muscle insertion points.
At the optic nerve head the sclera forms the lamina cribrosa through which retinal ganglion cell axons exit; elevated intraocular pressure damages this structure in glaucoma.
The sclera is a suspension element of the eye's optical system (FIO-01): it maintains the geometric shell within which the optical components are held in their correct spatial relationships.

The choroid is the highly vascular layer between the sclera and retina, supplying oxygen and nutrients to the outer retinal layers including the photoreceptors and retinal pigment epithelium, which are avascular.
The choriocapillaris beneath the macula has the highest blood flow per unit tissue of any structure in the body, reflecting the exceptional metabolic demands of macular photoreceptors.
The choroid is a metabolic suspension element of focus integrity (FIO-01): it sustains the living infrastructure of phototransduction.
Pattern recognition is a cognitive process by which the visual system identifies stimuli by matching sensory input against stored memory templates or prototypes.
Gestalt principles — proximity, similarity, continuity, closure — describe the brain's automatic tendencies to group visual elements into coherent patterns.
Pattern recognition operates at multiple processing levels simultaneously, including low-level feature detection and high-level categorical recognition.
In the Zonules FIO framework, pattern recognition is the primary site of FIO-05 Interpretation failures, where prior expectations distort the reading of incoming signal.
The visual system routinely fills in incomplete information, inferring object identity, surface continuity, and depth from partial cues.
Visual inference is governed by prior probabilities — the brain combines current sensory evidence with learned expectations to construct a most-likely percept.
Predictive coding models propose that the brain continuously generates predictions about incoming stimuli and only propagates prediction errors, not raw sensory data.
In the Zonules FIO framework, visual inference is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation because it introduces the risk that expectations override fidelity to the actual optical signal.
Perceptual load theory proposes that high-load tasks exhaust limited attentional capacity, suppressing processing of task-irrelevant stimuli automatically.
Under low perceptual load, spare cognitive capacity is allocated to distractors, increasing susceptibility to irrelevant stimulation.
Perceptual load interacts with selective attention, with high load producing more focused, effortless exclusion of competing visual information than low load conditions.
In the Zonules FIO framework, perceptual load maps to FIO-02 Accommodation: it defines the effective aperture of attentional focus available to any given visual scene.
Top-down processing uses stored knowledge, contextual expectations, and prior experience to guide interpretation of incoming visual signals, often overriding raw sensory evidence.
Context effects in visual perception — such as reading ambiguous letters differently depending on surrounding words — demonstrate the power of top-down priors.
Top-down and bottom-up processing interact continuously; neither operates in isolation in natural visual scenes, with each modulating the other across processing hierarchies.

In the Zonules FIO framework, top-down processing is the mechanism most directly implicated in FIO-05 Interpretation failures, as prior knowledge can systematically distort reading of an accurate signal.
Attention capture is the involuntary reorientation of visual attention driven by stimulus salience — features such as abrupt onset, high contrast, or sudden motion automatically recruit attention.
The distinction between goal-directed (endogenous) and stimulus-driven (exogenous) attention determines whether attentional shifts are voluntary or reflexively triggered by environmental events.
Salient distractors can capture attention even when observers are explicitly instructed to ignore them, demonstrating the automaticity of certain capture mechanisms.

In the Zonules FIO framework, attention capture is classified under FIO-03 Signal-Noise Separation: a salient but irrelevant stimulus generates noise that competes with the target signal for attentional resources.
Perceptual errors arise from systematic biases in sensory processing, context effects, prior expectations, and limits of neural computation, and are distinct from sensory noise.
Optical illusions are canonical demonstrations of perceptual error, where the visual system's heuristics for interpreting depth, size, and brightness produce demonstrably incorrect interpretations.
Perceptual errors are not random failures but often the predictable output of adaptively useful heuristics operating outside their domain of validity.
In the Zonules FIO framework, perceptual error is the observable output of FIO-05 Interpretation failure: the point where a well-transmitted signal is assigned an incorrect meaning.
Visual ambiguity occurs when a retinal image is consistent with more than one physical scene configuration, requiring the visual system to resolve it using priors or additional cues.
Reversible figures — such as the Necker cube or Rubin vase — are canonical demonstrations of perceptual ambiguity, where the same image alternates between two stable interpretations.

The visual system typically resolves ambiguity by selecting the most probable interpretation given learned priors, but this resolution can be unstable when competing interpretations are equally probable.

In the Zonules FIO framework, signal ambiguity is classified under FIO-03 Signal-Noise Separation: it represents the condition where signal definition is insufficient to support a stable, confident read.
Visual scene parsing involves simultaneous segmentation of figure from ground, grouping of elements into objects, and assignment of spatial relationships between objects.
Gist perception — the rapid, holistic extraction of a scene's category and dominant properties within the first 100 ms of viewing — precedes detailed object-level parsing.
Scene parsing is influenced by both bottom-up salience (contrast, motion, novelty) and top-down knowledge (scene schemas, expected object locations).

In the Zonules FIO framework, visual scene parsing is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: the act of assigning meaning and structure to a visual scene introduces interpretation-layer risk even when the optical signal is clean.
Optical flow describes the apparent motion of image brightness patterns across a sequence of frames, computed from frame-to-frame differences in pixel intensity.
Dense optical flow methods estimate a motion vector for every pixel in the image; sparse methods track selected feature points across frames.

Optical flow is subject to the aperture problem: local motion is inherently ambiguous without global context, because a moving edge seen through a small aperture is consistent with multiple motion directions.

In the Zonules FIO framework, optical flow is classified under FIO-01 Suspension: temporal coherence in motion estimation depends on maintaining a structural correspondence between frames that the aperture problem can undermine.
Scene understanding requires integrated operation of object detection, semantic segmentation, depth estimation, and relational reasoning to produce a globally consistent scene interpretation.
Scene understanding systems trained on standard benchmarks struggle to produce globally consistent interpretations under distribution shift, novel object categories, or unusual viewpoints.
Scene understanding performance degrades systematically under occlusion, rare object categories, and viewpoints not represented in training data.

In the Zonules FIO framework, scene understanding is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: it represents the highest-level integration task in machine vision, where failure to correctly interpret a scene persists even when individual components detect correctly.
Synthetic media encompasses outputs of generative systems including GAN-generated images, diffusion model outputs, NeRF renderings, and text-to-image synthesis.
The indistinguishability of synthetic media from authentic capture constitutes a provenance integrity failure: origin can no longer be inferred from appearance alone.
The C2PA standard provides cryptographically anchored metadata recording origin, edit history, and tool provenance, enabling authenticity verification independent of pixel analysis.

In the Zonules FIO framework, synthetic media is classified under FIO-04 Provenance: the structural challenge is that appearance alone cannot establish origin, requiring an independent provenance chain.
Visual grounding requires identifying the image region referred to by a natural-language expression, linking linguistic semantics to spatial location in the image.
Referring expression comprehension is the canonical visual grounding task, requiring a model to localize a unique referent described by a natural-language phrase.

Visual grounding benchmarks reveal gaps in compositional understanding: models can succeed on individual words while failing on multi-attribute expressions requiring joint spatial and categorical reasoning.

In the Zonules FIO framework, visual grounding is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: it requires the system to produce a correct interpretation of how language and image jointly specify a region.
Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel in an image, partitioning the image into regions corresponding to recognized categories.
Instance segmentation extends semantic segmentation by distinguishing individual instances of the same class, assigning each pixel both a category label and an instance identity.
Semantic segmentation fails silently on out-of-vocabulary regions: pixels belonging to unrecognized categories are assigned to the nearest known class rather than flagged as unknown.

In the Zonules FIO framework, semantic segmentation is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: assigning meaning to every pixel is an act of interpretation that can fail systematically without signalling failure.
Ophthalmic AI systems have demonstrated diagnostic performance comparable to specialist clinicians on specific grading tasks such as diabetic retinopathy screening from fundus photographs.
Ophthalmic AI performance is sensitive to acquisition device, image quality, patient demographics, and disease prevalence, with performance varying substantially across deployment contexts.
Regulatory clearance for ophthalmic AI requires demonstration of safety and effectiveness across diverse populations and acquisition conditions beyond benchmark performance.
In the Zonules FIO framework, ophthalmic AI is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: the system's task is to interpret retinal images, and interpretation failures carry direct clinical consequence.
Medical imaging AI has achieved expert-level benchmark performance across several modalities including radiology, pathology, and ophthalmology, on specific task-benchmark combinations.

Performance gaps between benchmark conditions and clinical deployment have been documented across medical imaging AI, driven by distribution shift, patient population differences, and workflow integration factors.
Medical imaging AI outputs require integration with clinical context for appropriate use; autonomous AI decision-making in diagnosis is not supported by most current regulatory frameworks.

In the Zonules FIO framework, medical imaging AI is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: translating image data into clinical meaning is the interpretation task, and the gap between benchmark and deployment is the interpretation failure mode.
Neural network interpretability methods include feature visualization, saliency mapping, activation analysis, probing classifiers, and mechanistic circuit analysis.
Attribution methods such as Grad-CAM generate pixel-level explanations of model decisions by computing gradients with respect to feature map activations.
High-accuracy models can rely on spurious correlations in training data, achieving correct benchmark performance for incorrect reasons that fail under distribution shift.

In the Zonules FIO framework, neural network interpretability is classified under FIO-05 Interpretation: interpretability methods exist because the model's internal interpretation of its input cannot be assumed correct even when its output is.
Retinal ganglion cells are the output neurons of the retina whose axons form the optic nerve and carry visual signals to the brain.
They receive processed input from bipolar and amacrine cells and encode features such as contrast and motion before transmission.
Distinct ganglion cell populations, including magnocellular and parvocellular pathways, convey different aspects of the visual signal.
Retinal ganglion cells are the interpretation gateway of the eye (FIO-05): the final retinal encoding stage that determines what reaches the brain.
The blind spot, or optic disc, is the region of the retina where the optic nerve exits and which contains no photoreceptors, producing a gap in the visual field.
Because the optic disc lacks photoreceptors, no visual signal originates from that location.
The visual system normally fills in the blind spot so that the gap is not consciously perceived.
The blind spot is a provenance gap (FIO-04): a region where the perceived image is constructed rather than recorded, with no underlying signal of origin.
Saccades are rapid ballistic eye movements that shift the fovea from one point of interest to another.
During a saccade visual sensitivity is briefly reduced, a phenomenon called saccadic suppression.
Saccades reposition the high-acuity fovea because detailed vision is limited to a small central region of the retina.
Saccades are an accommodation mechanism of gaze (FIO-02): they repeatedly re-aim the eye to hold focus on changing targets.
Smooth pursuit is a voluntary eye movement that tracks a moving target to keep its image stable on the fovea.
Unlike saccades, smooth pursuit continuously matches eye velocity to target velocity.
Smooth pursuit generally requires a moving stimulus and cannot be sustained over a stationary scene.
Smooth pursuit is a continuous accommodation of gaze (FIO-02): focus held on a target across its motion.
The extraocular muscles are the six muscles that rotate each eye within the orbit to direct gaze.
They comprise four rectus and two oblique muscles, each producing characteristic eye rotations.
Coordinated action of the extraocular muscles of both eyes maintains binocular alignment.
The extraocular muscles are the actuators of the accommodation-of-gaze system (FIO-02): the mechanism that physically re-aims focus.
The optic radiations are the white-matter fiber tracts carrying visual signals from the lateral geniculate nucleus to the primary visual cortex.
Fibers serving different parts of the visual field follow distinct trajectories, including the temporal detour known as Meyer's loop.
Damage to the optic radiations produces characteristic visual field deficits that localize the lesion.
The optic radiations are a suspension pathway (FIO-01): structure that holds the signal's spatial organization en route to cortex.
The retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is a single layer of pigmented cells between the photoreceptors and the choroid that supports photoreceptor function.
The RPE recycles visual pigment, phagocytoses shed photoreceptor outer segments, and absorbs stray light.
RPE dysfunction is implicated in retinal degenerations such as age-related macular degeneration.
The RPE is a maintenance-suspension layer (FIO-01): hidden support that keeps the photoreceptors operating.
The conjunctiva is the thin transparent mucous membrane covering the sclera and lining the inner eyelids.
It contributes to the tear film and protects the ocular surface from debris and infection.
Inflammation of the conjunctiva, called conjunctivitis, is a common ocular surface condition.
The conjunctiva is a signal-noise boundary (FIO-03): it keeps the ocular surface clear so the optical path stays uncorrupted.
The trabecular meshwork is the spongy tissue at the iridocorneal angle through which aqueous humor drains from the eye.
It regulates aqueous outflow and thereby helps maintain intraocular pressure.
Impaired outflow through the trabecular meshwork is a major mechanism of open-angle glaucoma.
The trabecular meshwork is a pressure-suspension element (FIO-01): hidden regulation that keeps the globe's geometry stable.
Ocular fixation is the maintenance of gaze on a single location, holding the target's image on the fovea.
Even during fixation the eyes make tiny involuntary movements: microsaccades, drift, and tremor.
These fixational eye movements prevent the perceptual fading of a stabilized image.
Fixation is the ocular suspension of focus (FIO-01): active stabilization that holds the focal target in place.
The tear film is the thin fluid layer coating the ocular surface that maintains a smooth optical surface on the cornea.
It has lipid, aqueous, and mucin components that together lubricate, nourish, and protect the surface.
An unstable or deficient tear film degrades the corneal optical surface and visual quality, as in dry eye.
The tear film is a signal-noise layer (FIO-03): the first optical surface, kept clear so light enters uncorrupted.
